data.title: Google: Holding A High Market Share In Search
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Baron Fifth Avenue Growth Fund Q3 2025 Shareholder Letter.

Baron Fifth Avenue Growth Fund continued adding to the parent company of Google, the world’s largest search and online advertising company, Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL). While the company continues to hold high market share in search, it also owns the world’s leading video platform, YouTube, a leading hyperscaler, Google Cloud, a leading ad network, and optionality in a number of smaller subsidiaries like the autonomous vehicle company, Waymo.

Generative AI models like ChatGPT have introduced a bear case for Alphabet for the first time in over 20 years, centering on the potential disruption of its core search business and massive advertising revenue stream. While Google has long held a near-monopolistic position in the search engine market, the rise of LLMs like ChatGPT could lead to a shift in user behavior and a potential erosion of Google’s long-standing lead. For long-term investors, the central risk is that generative AI-powered interfaces could fundamentally change how users seek information, bypassing traditional search results and threatening Alphabet’s most profitable advertising engine. However, Google has thus far only seen growth in the number of search queries supported by AI overviews and AI mode, which have also expanded the addressable opportunity to longer and more complex queries. Additionally, Google has seen paid clicks reaccelerate despite the rise of ChatGPT.

More broadly, the bull case for Alphabet is multifaceted, leveraging its extensive AI expertise and vast ecosystem. Firstly, Alphabet’s DeepMind is a leader in AI research, with a superior talent pipeline and retention, allowing the company to attract top-tier talent and convert research into products at scale – see the recent Veo 3 video generation model release or the significant progress that Alphabet has made with its Gemini models. Second, in contrast to rivals like Meta, Alphabet’s diversified revenue streams and hardware investments, such as its custom tensor processing units, provide a crucial advantage. By developing its own full-stack AI infrastructure, from hardware to applications, Alphabet gains superior price-to-performance relative to general-purpose chips like GPUs. This approach enables a more cost-efficient scaling of AI workloads all else being equal. Third, Alphabet’s scaled user aggregation surfaces, offers a significant competitive moat. The company has a “billion user club,” with nine software products boasting over a billion users each, including Android, Chrome, YouTube, Gmail, and Google Maps. This massive, existing user base provides a substantial advantage by effectively lowering customer acquisition costs for new AI products. Critically, this scaled user base is a powerful magnet for developers, who are incentivized to build applications that integrate with Google’s AI engine to reach a vast audience. This flywheel effect – where users attract developers, and a robust app ecosystem attracts more users – strengthens Alphabet’s position at the heart of the AI landscape. A fourth forward-looking driver is the coming evolution in commerce, in which every piece of content becomes “shoppable.” With its scaled services like YouTube and Google Maps, combined with advanced AI, Alphabet is uniquely positioned to capitalize on this shift. For instance, AI models could recognize products in real-time within videos or images, and via integrations with merchants, enable them to be shoppable just in time – this should strengthen Alphabet’s positioning in commerce.

Lastly, the recent resolution in the Department of Justice case against Alphabet, which was close to a best-case scenario, removes a significant overhang from the stock. Getting everything Alphabet has to offer across its platform and ecosystem at a 23 times P/E multiple is attractive for long-term investors in our view.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses of the investment carefully before investing. The prospectus and summary prospectus contain this and other information about the Funds. You may obtain them from the Funds’ distributor, Baron Capital, Inc., by calling 1-800-99-BARON or visiting BaronCapitalGroup.com. Please read them carefully before investing.

Risks: The Fund invests primarily in large cap equity securities which are subject to price fluctuations in the stock market. Even though the Fund is diversified, it may establish significant positions where the Adviser has the greatest conviction. This could increase volatility of the Fund’s returns.

The Fund may not achieve its objectives. Portfolio holdings are subject to change. Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to risk.

The discussions of the companies herein are not intended as advice to any person regarding the advisability of investing in any particular security. The views expressed in this report reflect those of the respective portfolio managers only through the end of the period stated in this report. The portfolio manager’s views are not intended as recommendations or investment advice to any person reading this report and are subject to change at any time based on market and other conditions and Baron has no obligation to update them.

This report does not constitute an offer to sell or a solicitation of any offer to buy securities of Baron Fifth Avenue Growth Fund by anyone in any jurisdiction where it would be unlawful under the laws of that jurisdiction to make such offer or solicitation.

Alpha measures the difference between a fund’s actual returns and its expected performance, given its level of risk as measured by beta. Beta explains common variation in stock returns due to different stock sensitivities to market or systematic risk that cannot be explained by the US Country factor. Positive exposure indicates high beta stock. Negative exposure indicates low beta stock. Price/Earnings Ratio or P/E (next 12 months): is a valuation ratio of a company’s current share price compared to its mean forecasted 4-quarter sum earnings per share over the next twelve months. If a company’s EPS estimate is negative, it is excluded from the portfolio-level calculation. EPS Growth Rate (3-5-year forecast) indicates the long term forecasted EPS growth of the companies in the portfolio, calculated using the weighted average of the available 3-to-5 year forecasted growth rates for each of the stocks in the portfolio provided by FactSet Estimates. The EPS Growth rate does not forecast the Fund’s performance.

data.title: Harrison Schwartz: Why PPLT Is My Top Platinum ETF Pick Through 2030
data.description:
Follow Harrison Schwartz on Seeking Alpha!

Follow PPLT on Seeking Alpha!

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Content is offered for information purposes only. Unless stated otherwise, any and all individuals participating in the video are third parties that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body. Unless stated otherwise, the views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole.

The accuracy and completeness of content shared cannot be guaranteed. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, US investment adviser, or investment bank.

Daniel Snyder: Hey everyone. Welcome back to another episode of ETF Spotlight. I'm Daniel Snyder from Seeking Alpha. Today, we are lucky enough to be talking to Harrison Schwartz. He is going to take us into the world of platinum and palladium and what his opinions are in the space right now.

Obviously, lots of focus on gold and silver and those commodities through the years, specifically more recently with a lot of worry out there. Maybe it's inflation, maybe it's debt, maybe it's government shutdown, whatever it might be, right? But platinum and palladium have been these silent lurkers over the years, and we're excited to jump into the conversation with him. So, obviously, we're going to jump into an ETF.

And today, that will be PPLT. That's abrdn’s Physical Platinum Shares ETF. And, Harrison, I would love to hear the case of one, first, you've been writing on Seeking Alpha since 2018. How did you get into specifically these metals?

Harrison Schwartz: So, I've always, for me, it's always been about how do we defend the portfolio? How do we make a portfolio that's robust against any situation that – any all-weather. Right? So, I always like alternatives. I'm always looking for alternatives because you get low correlations, and if you have a lot of things with low correlations, that lowers portfolio volatility.

Platinum, for me, is great because it has relatively low correlations, but it gives you exposure to hedges against monetary risk. So, dollar devaluation hedge, like gold, but under the radar. And I think that's really valuable because you get some more potential for alpha because it's not seen by most people. And so, I look mostly for alternatives. I think platinum and palladium are very interesting for those reasons.

DS: Well, so let's talk about this. Because I know most people think about let's take gold, for example, we just mentioned gold. There's demand from central banks from around the world. Investors can understand, okay, there's the buyer, there's the constant demand. Where does the demand come from for platinum and palladium? What's the end product, or what's the usage, or what's the store of value, and how does that market shape out?

HS: Platinum is interesting, because it's much like silver, where you get a mix of industrial demand and investment demand. There's not that central bank demand, which is something that maybe we'll see change as the precious metals boom goes on, but we have a mixture of industrial demand, more industrial demand with platinum than we see with silver. So that gives you some exposure to the positive and negatives of the economy while also having a big hedge against inflation. Because ultimately, if things really drive up, all the precious metals will go up probably similarly, not just gold.

DS: Alright. So, what about, let's talk about the supply aspect of it, right? We know how hard it is to mine gold and it adds to the source of value there, but is platinum and palladium, are those easier to mine? Where are they mined? What's the backdrop of the geography?

HS: So platinum, what's interesting is, it's somewhere around a 30th as much abundant as gold. So, there's far less platinum in the ground than gold. It’s far less produced every year than gold. And it's mostly produced in South Africa. It's mostly produced by one company, Sibanye-Stillwater. So, that gives you, one, if anything happens to these few mines in the world that produce it, that could mess up supply. And we're seeing that right now where the company, Sibanye-Stillwater, is cutting production because of lower prices, and that alone is enough to probably drive up a shortage of the metal. And on top of rising investment demand, I think that gives you a good catalyst for a long-term breakout of sustained shortage even if industrial demand does not keep up.

DS: So, you mentioned Sibanye-Stillwater Limited, that's ticker symbol SBSW. Are they like the power player of the market? Are they the OPEC PLUS of this realm?

HS: They really are because you get – the specific numbers I think people might want to look up, but I think it's around a quarter of all production comes from this one company. So, they have a huge amount of movement in the market for both platinum and palladium. And South Africa in general is very concentrated in that market. So, if you see tariffs, if you see any trade issues, all of a sudden, how are we going to get this metal? And where else does it come from? Russia. So, it's not seen in great abundance outside of countries where the trade stability is a little bit less strong than we usually see with other metals.

DS: Now, you had mentioned that the Platinum ETF, PPLT is actually one of your top investment picks all the way up to 2030. Why do you have the conviction to go that far in advance? I mean, it's almost just only 2026 right now, right? So, you're talking about multiyear call here.

HS: Yeah. And, truthfully, my bullish conviction has not changed much over the past five years or however long I've been looking at this. Because, to me, as an investor, I always want to look long-term. I always want to think, like, I think you can make the most alpha by thinking 10 to 20 years in the future. So, 10 years in the future, we know that there's going to be some difficulty with financing the government. We're seeing more dovish monetary policies or quick cuts of interest rates, things like that, which could definitely push inflation a bit higher than we've seen over the past 20 years or past 40 years, really.

And so, I think long-term I expect higher demand for investments for inflation hedges. Platinum, I think, is a really good pick for your retail investor, not necessarily your central bank. So, I think there's a great deal of upside there.

DS: Now, what do you think about, you had added this great chart in your last article on this ETF. A comparison between the gold price and platinum price, right? And if I just look at the chart, I can see, like, there's a massive spike back during the GFC and then a massive dip and, like, crazy volatility all the way, it looks up until, like, 2011, 2012, 2013 maybe. And then a steady pullback over the years where we haven't even surpassed that all-time high. So, do you feel like we're going back to all-time highs? Are we going back and then soaring above them? Are we going to go back and consolidate there? What are your thoughts?

HS: So, truthfully, I think the road back to that parity ratio we saw in the 2000s, may be slow because since then there's been more EVs, less demand for diesel vehicles. So, that lowers industrial demand in the short-term. I think that we're seeing more industrial demand growth from medical and other, like, hydrogen fuel cells, other technologies that we're seeing industrial demand level off and stabilize, not grow sharply.

I really think the growth is going to be from the investment side, and that remains speculative because that depends on having more monetary volatility that we've seen over the past year. Though I think that's likely.

DS: So, your case, if I understand, and please correct me if I'm wrong, is, the price of platinum, palladium will go up because it will be a store of value similar to gold, right, which people traditionally flock to. Nowadays, we also have Bitcoin as well, right? So, do you feel like, I mean, is there a case, it seems like people want to consolidate into one, maybe two sources of value in times of trouble. I mean, why do we start diversifying three, four, five? And then, do you get what I'm saying, where if you lose the consolidation, you might lose the additional value on top of it?

HS: Well, the way I'd see it is, we know a lot of companies need platinum for industrial uses. So, inflation rises, the cost of producing it will rise, as we've seen. And if the cost of producing it rises, then less will be produced if the price doesn't rise too. So, I think that there's no reason to say, we need to go on one thing only. Anything that we're seeing demand is existing and the cost of production is rising with inflation, with global inflation, that will ultimately push things up.

Whether or not, gold, I can see – gold should move first, because gold is the most robust hedge against inflation, but your other metals, like platinum, palladium, even copper, all in the long-term should rise according to their cost of production. And really, you're hedging an increase in cost of production primarily.

DS: Alright. So, let's talk to investors real quick before we start to wrap things up here. You had mentioned, this is mainly found in Russia. It's mainly found in South Africa. Like, to me, that sounds risky, right? Is that the biggest risk for this space, or are there other risks that you also think about in the back of your mind?

HS: Bullish or bearish risks?

DS: Let's go with both.

HS: Okay. So, I definitely think on the supply side, absolutely, there's that trade risk and production risk with either of those two areas. And that's bullish as an investor because that supply is, and it seems like the strategic metals or rare earth metals, once you don't have that supply, all of a sudden, those prices go right up. So, that's a, supply side risk is bullish. Risk - so, of course, if we see a change in policy and fiscal monetary policy that, is really strong to defend the U.S. dollar and push inflation down, that would moderate the trend in precious metals. I don't think that's likely, but that's where I would say is the greatest risk in all precious metals right now, especially because we've seen prices rise so much in gold.

DS: Alright. So, let me ask you this. I'm looking at PPLT here, the ETF on Seeking Alpha. And I noticed that really the price increase started around May 20th, beginning June this year. What has been happening since that moment? Because tariffs were in April. Is that when South Africa and tariffs dispute happened? What was the event there where this started to increase? I think it's up 71% year to date.

HS: I think the market is realizing that there is a supply shortage in the short-term and probably the long-term. We've seen Sibanye-Stillwater production cuts for platinum and palladium due to low prices, primarily for palladium, but that lowers production for platinum because they're mined together.

So, I think that that alone is driving higher prices, and then we're seeing more market realization of this as a long-term trend on top of higher investment demand. Gold soared since last year, and now people are saying, oh, hey, what else can I get into that might have similar profile to gold? Platinum's a great choice. Decent choice. I would say it's a great choice with more industrial risk.

DS: Well, so let's go with that. Great choice versus decent choice. It also leads me to think about how much of a portfolio allocation should be within this space. So, what would you say? I mean, is this like a 5% BlackRock - , Crypto, 5%, like, 5%, 10%, 15%? Or you're you saying let's swing the bat and let's go 30%, 35%?

HS: The one thing I'd say is, the cost of production of platinum is very close to its current price. So, for me, the long-term downside risk is pretty low. I'll be surprised to see prices go lower than they had been last year. So that gives you low risk that could allow for a higher portfolio position. For me, I would keep the lower portfolio position diversifying a few metals. This is, I think, the largest metal position I would have because I think it's undervalued, compared to the other metals, but, yeah, 5%, 15%, probably no more than 15%.

DS: Alright, Harrison. Let's go ahead and wrap it up there. Everyone, PPLT, this is the platinum and palladium side of things. Obviously, go follow Harrison Schwartz here on Seeking Alpha. Harrison, I hope you continue to write about this space. I find it fascinating. I know many others do as well. Thank you for joining the program this week. Everyone, this has been another excellent episode of ETF Spotlight, and we'll see you again here next week. Take care.

Follow Harrison Schwartz on Seeking Alpha!

Follow PPLT on Seeking Alpha!

data.title: Nvidia Stock Forecast: The Path To $6 Trillion
data.description:
Two years ago, Nvidia Corporation's (NVDA) April 2023 quarter delivered a historic 18% beat, followed by an even bigger 30% beat in July 2023. Compare that to the most recent quarter ending July 2025 — just a 4% beat, the smallest in two years. The narrowing beats combined with a rare QoQ decline in the Compute segment could be interpreted as Nvidia is running out of steam.

I am frequently asked by notable media anchors if Nvidia could be topping, and as you’ll see below, I substantiate why Nvidia is not running out of steam. I spoke with Charles Payne from Fox Business and Caroline Hyde from Bloomberg, followed by a 45-minute interview on the topic with Maggie Lake of Wealthion. In these conversations, we discussed some of the most pressing issues that AI investors face – does China revenue matter for Nvidia, how does the world’s most valuable company continue to grow, are we in an AI bubble, and lastly – where is we are positioning next.

For those new to my firm, the Tech Insider Network is among the top-performing AI portfolios globally. Our track record was built in part by taking a significant Nvidia position years before the market embraced the AI story, with allocations as high as 10–20%. While our calls on Nvidia are well recognized, we’ve also held a number of lesser-known AI winners that together have driven a cumulative return of 210%. To put that in perspective, if we were a hedge fund, we’d rank #2 — and if we were an ETF, we’d rank #5.

What that ranking should communicate to you is that the Tech Insider Network does not rest on our laurels, we rigorously pursue a portfolio positioning that will outperform. Let me rephrase this to say that my firm will not continue to hold Nvidia out of fandom, rather, we will continue to hold Nvidia only if we believe the stock continues to offer alpha.

Below, I explain why this stock has the potential to outperform the indexes — and how we’re positioning to capture even greater returns from lesser-known stocks that are riding Nvidia’s momentum.

Is There Alpha Left In Nvidia Stock?

After being crowned the world’s most valuable company with a market cap of over $4 trillion, it's natural to wonder if the easy money has already been made. For investors, the debate comes down to whether Nvidia can keep outpacing both the Nasdaq and its semiconductor peers, given its phenomenal run.

Regardless of market fluctuations, Nvidia’s product road map is not slowing down. As described in the videos below, Nvidia is on “the eve of releasing its next generation of GPUs,” combined with its enviable software platform CUDA and strong QoQ growth in AI networking (NVLink, Spectrum-X, InfiniBand).

In the interview below with Charles Payne of Fox Business, which took place ahead of earnings, I offer key reasons that Nvidia continues to offer alpha as we head into calendar year 2026 and why we plan to buy the stock on any dips.

I also had the opportunity to talk to Maggie Lake of Wealthion on the importance of Nvidia and why the company is moving from its 2.0 era to the 3.0 era, and how this transition from server-scale to a rack-scale AI systems company is key as to why Nvidia has further room to run. In the clip, I also describe the opportunity that Nvidia is poised to capture that will be as large as the AI hardware opportunity.

The AI Bubble: Fact Or Fiction

Fears as to whether AI is reaching bubble territory were ignited this past month when OpenAI (OPENAI) CEO Sam Altman compared AI to the dot-com era, stating:

“When bubbles happen, smart people get overexcited about a kernel of truth. If you look at most of the bubbles in history, like the tech bubble, there was a real thing. Tech was really important. The internet was a really big deal. People got overexcited.”

Given that OpenAI is seeing about $20 billion in revenue as of now with no profits, it makes sense that Altman would be concerned about valuations. However, a lack of profits is certainly not Nvidia’s concern. The company had a GAAP operating margin of 60.8% in Q2 and an adjusted operating margin of 64.5% with over $13 billion in cash flow for the quarter.

There is clearly a bifurcation in the AI economy, as some companies must spend heavily to compete, while others are profiting heavily from the steep competition.

In as lucid of a manner as possible, I break down in this video the following:

  • Why technically every tech trend goes through a boom/bust, why this should not scare investors off, plus the strategy investors can use to successfully win with surging tech trends.
  • What companies are more insulated from the effects of a bubble, and why AI software like OpenAI is at higher risk.
  • The differences between an enterprise technology and a consumer technology in terms of how AI will repay Big Tech capex.

Nvidia's Stock Can Reach $6 Trillion Market Cap By Next Year

In an interview that went viral with Caroline Hyde of Bloomberg, I discussed why it’s important to not focus too closely on quarterly earnings reports for determining Nvidia’s growth potential, and to rather focus on GPU generations.

If we look at the revenue potential for quarterly data center revenue once Blackwell and Blackwell Ultra ship in volume, this leads us to a $6 trillion valuation by next year.

Predicting 50% upside may not seem like much given Nvidia is up 325% since early 2024 and up 4,000%+ since my firm first bought the stock in 2018, however, there is a chance investors have an opportunity to get Nvidia lower.

For example, as recent as January of this year, my firm predicted Nvidia would dip below $100 – this was published before the DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) news hit. This afforded our followers a better entry, leading to higher upside. Next week, we will be updating our buy plan for Nvidia for free.

The Inevitable AI Trend That Will Profit Most from Nvidia's Upcoming Generation Of GPUs

Nvidia 1.0 was defined by the company's lead in gaming, whereas Nvidia 2.0 was defined by the introduction of Tensor Cores with Volta. These cores evolved through Ampere and the Hopper generation, ultimately leading to the transformer engine and more precision support for accelerating AI compute. The market eventually caught onto the enormity of the AI market as Hopper facilitated more accurate GenAI applications coming to market. You can read more about this history in this analysis: “Here’s Why Nvidia Will Reach a $10 Trillion Market Cap by 2030.”

Looking forward, what I am dubbing as “Nvidia 3.0” will be the shift from 8-GPU server-scale systems to 72-GPU rack-scale systems — which is augmented with the back-to-back release of Blackwell and Blackwell Ultra. Nvidia’s new rack-scale systems will mark one of the most significant turning points in its history. Regardless of what market participants think about Big Tech’s surging capex spend, the spend will continue to rise as those who get the NVL72 systems (that are beginning to ramp now) will have a critical advantage over those still on the HGX or DGX systems with 8 GPUs.

There is one inevitable trend that will profit most from the upcoming generation of GPUs, with significant momentum from Nvidia’s new rack-system architectures. This 5X to 9X opportunity that is already beginning to take shape with material evidence of the momentum.

Don’t miss out on what a leading AI portfolio and analyst team is saying about where to position next to take advantage of AI’s next major move.

AI Networking Is The #1 Opportunity From Nvidia's Upcoming GPUs

Under the hood, what defines the upcoming generation of GPUs from the previous generation is AI networking. When you move from 8 GPU severs to 72 GPU rack-scale systems, your intra-rack networking shifts, as does the rack-to-rack for larger clusters due to higher GPU density. For example, we’ve seen hyperscalers plan to deploy 1 million GPU clusters by 2027-2028, up from clusters with 10s of thousands right now. Therefore, both scale-up and scale-out architecture are going through a rapid transformation on the networking side.

In this video, I discuss why the Tech Insider Network is concentrated in AI networking stocks as our #1 trend right now:

Conclusion:

Nvidia and its ecosystem of suppliers are the best way to position for the rest of 2025. While headlines were fixated on China, the bigger story is clear: H20s contributed about $4B, while Blackwell generated an estimated $28B this past quarter — over a $100B run rate. This is why my stance has been to focus on GPU generations to track the AI opportunity rather than quarterly earnings or any single geographic area for seven years and counting. Semiconductors are particularly tricky, as the cyclical bottom can often be the best time to buy.

Keep an eye out for the Tech Insider Network's new buy plan for Nvidia, hitting inboxes next week. Our last buy plan provided an entry under $100 when the market felt the stock was invincible. This led to entries with real-time trade alerts at $94.48 and $87.99, with real-time trade alerts sent to our Premium Members.

For 2025, the Tech Insider Network has worked to identify key Nvidia suppliers, with Blackwell on deck to ramp significantly, sharing our in-depth research on stocks within the AI networking stack. We hold high allocations in two specific Nvidia AI networking suppliers.

Please note: The Tech Insider Network conducts research and draws conclusions for the company’s portfolio. We then share that information with our readers and offer real-time trade notifications. This is not a guarantee of a stock’s performance, and it is not financial advice. Please consult your personal financial advisor before buying any stock in the companies mentioned in this analysis. Beth Kindig and the Tech Insider Network own shares in NVDA at the time of writing, and may own stocks pictured in the charts.

data.title: Elanco Animal Health: What A Difference An Innovation Year Makes
data.description:
Back in May, I concluded that deleveraging takes time in the case of Elanco Animal Health (ELAN). The company and stock has been hurt by pipeline delays, debt incurred from the deal with Bayer and generally underwhelming growth, making that progress was painfully slow.

However, fast forwarding half a year in time, shares have risen some 80%, having risen from $12 to $22 per share. This move higher was driven by debt concerns moving definitively to the background, as the big driver is that the innovation part of the business is on fire. This has driven a well-deserved re-rating in the stock here.

Enriching Life With Animal Health

Elanco was the former animal health business from Eli Lilly (LLY) until that parent company decided to spin off this business already some 7 years ago, following the successful spin-off from Zoetis (ZTS) by Pfizer a couple of years before.

The company has a mission to help pets to live longer, healthier and more active lives. On the other item of the spectrum, the company helps farmers to improve animal health, wellbeing and raise livestock in a more sustainable manner.

For a long time, shares traded in a $20-$30 range, but this changed when the company acquired the Animal Health business from Bayer in a big deal. That transaction would add $1.7 billion in sales, with Elanco paying a 4.5 times sales multiples for these activities, being key to grow Elanco to the business which it currently is.

The promise was to create a business set to generate a billion in EBITDA, but that never materialized as the debt overhang created a real overhang in the years following, pressuring shares as well. This made that shares fell to the $10 mark in 2023, levels at which shares traded again earlier this year.

For the year 2024, the company generated $4.4 billion in sales with EBITDA seen just over $900 million, and while this missed the promises, these margins surpassed 20% of sales. Its activities, including over 200 brands, employ nearly 10,000 workers, serving customers in nearly 100 countries, covering over a dozen of manufacturing sites in its network as well. Revenues were split pretty evenly between pet health and livestock products, with the business having an even split between domestic and international activities.

Valuation Struggles

After the company grew 2021 sales to nearly $4.8 billion, revenues fell back to $4.4 billion in 2022 and 2023, with falling EBITDA metrics making that relative and absolute leverage was on the increase. Trying to tackle this, Elanco sold its aqua business last year in a $1.3 billion deal, for over a 7 times sales multiple, allowing the business to reduce net debt towards $4.5 billion, for leverage ratios still seen around 5 times EBITDA.

2024 sales rose by 0.5% to $4.44 billion, yet if we correct for the divestment of the aqua assets, revenues were up 3%. EBITDA was down 7% to $910 million, with adjusted earnings up two pennies to $0.91 per share. With net debt down to $3.9 billion and the company guiding for 2025 sales a midpoint of $4.48 billion, stability looked in sight. However, EBITDA was seen down to $850 million, hurt by the high-margins divestment of the aqua business.

However, there were more green shoots, as the company sold its royalties and commercial milestones related to XDEMVY in a $295 million cash deal, a useful amount to reduce debt further. Following a 4% increase in first quarter organic sales, the midpoint of the sales guidance was hiked to $4.545 billion, yet EBITDA and earnings estimates were kept intact.

Trading down to $12, the half a billion shares value equity of the business at $6 billion, for a $10 billion enterprise valuation. This is equal to about 2 times sales, a low-teens EBITDA multiple and 14-15 times earnings seen in the low-eighties cents per share. Still holding a modest long position, I felt hard-pressed to increase that position, but simultaneously saw no need to get out of the position as well.

Making Progress

In August, Elanco reported a solid 8% increase in second quarter organic sales, with revenues reported up 5% to $1.24 billion. This looked better than it is, with adjusted EBITDA down 13% to $238 million and adjusted earnings down four cents to $0.26 per share, as net debt came down to $3.4 billion.

The company hiked the full year sales guidance to a midpoint of $4.595 billion and with EBITDA seen around $870 million, the midpoint of the earnings guidance has been increased to $0.88 per share. The improved guidance is attributed to accelerating innovation revenues, now seen between $720-$800 million, up sixty million from the previous guidance.

More good news arrived in September, when Elanco announced that Credelio Quattro was reaching a $100 million sales number, as soon as 8 months after its launch. Later that month, the FDA approved an improved Zenrelia label, used for control of itching and inflammation associated with skin allergies for dogs.

Early in November, Elanco reported a 10% increase in third quarter sales to $1.14 billion, with organic growth accelerating to 9%. With the aqua divestment lapsing, EBITDA rose by 21% to $198 million, with adjusted earnings up six cents to $0.19 per share.

Net debt came down to just below $3.3 billion, as momentum made that the revenue guidance was hiked to a midpoint of $4.66 billion, with EBITDA now seen between $880-$900 million, and adjusted earnings seen between $0.91 and $0.94 per share. By now, net leverage ratios have come in below 4 times, as realistic earnings have improved a bit since the start of the year, but this is really only about ten cents.

Sentiment Has Shift

The return to organic growth is shifting the balance here, combined with a reduction in leverage, making that any debt concerns are rapidly moving to the background here. The company is seeing accelerating organic growth, with the innovation sales guidance hiked by another $100 million to $840-$880 million, with new launches making a huge impact on the business.

With nearly 500 million shares outstanding, these shares have risen some ten dollars to $22 per share, for an $11 billion equity valuation and now about $14 billion enterprise valuation. This pushes up the valuation to nearly 3 times sales, with the business valued around 16 times EBITDA and equity trading at multiples in the mid-twenties.

This accelerating momentum is driving the shares and expanding valuation multiples here. Frankly, there is quite some momentum here, with organic growth accelerating to nearly 10% here, yet despite this impressive growth, EBITDA margins are actually down about one and a half points so far this year.

This just shows the potential. After all, a percent increase in margins on this revenue base could boost pre-tax earnings by ten cents, leaving real potential if continued growth will be accompanied by margin gains from here onwards as well, creating a very credible roadmap for earnings to top the dollar per share mark in 2026, combined with solid organic growth.

However, by now the performance has been priced into the shares already, as the big 80% rally since May has pushed up valuation multiples quite a bit, in between accelerating organic growth and debt reduction. Right now, I am still happy with a modest long position, although kicking myself for not finding commitment to buy more, but I feel no need to chase the shares here, nor to take profits yet.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Moving Forward At A Good Pace
data.description:
December 1, 2025, is the date we are waiting for this year.

Starting with December 1, the Federal Reserve has said that it will begin a new monetary policy.

The Fed has been on the current monetary agenda since the middle of March 2022, with a policy of quantitative tightening.

During that time period, the Fed has reduced its securities portfolio by $2,203.5 billion...or by $2.2 trillion!

This represents almost 25 percent of the securities held outright at the beginning of this time.

The Fed has been very steady and persistent in sticking with its policy...a truly remarkable feat.

Here is what the movement in the securities portfolio looks like.

This period of quantitative tightening was in response to the period of time when the Fed was reacting to the spread of the COVID-19 pandemic and was pumping lots and lots of money into the commercial banking system.

To get a feel for what the Fed was doing during the spread of the COVID-19 pandemic, we can go to this chart and see how the M2 money stock was expanding during the early part of the 2020s.

Chairman Powell and the Federal Reserve leadership acted in the early 20s to protect the U.S. economy from a more severe economic collapse. They were content to "err" on the side of monetary ease so as to protect the economy.

However, as the spread of the pandemic came to an end, the Fed was stuck with the problem of reducing the amount of "money" it had injected into the banking system and making the commercial banks a little less liquid.

What the two charts show, however, is that whereas the commercial banking system had plenty of bank reserves and could underwrite a fairly rapid increase in the U.S. money stock, the growth in the money stock could not be stopped even though the Federal Reserve was reducing its securities portfolio as rapidly as it was doing.

The latest data on the M2 money stock is for early October 2025.

The compound growth of the money stock, from October 2023 to October 2025, was just under 4.0 percent.

The compound growth rate of the money stock from October 2019 to October 2025 was just under 7.0 percent.

With the money stock growing at this pace, the economy could certainly maintain a relatively strong rate of growth even though the Federal Reserve was actually reducing the size of the Fed's securities portfolio.

That is, we had more than three and one-half years of monetary tightening...yet the money stock rose rapidly enough for the economy to rise in the 3.0 percent to 2.5 percent range for the whole period.

See the chart below.

Conclusion: even though the Federal Reserve was removing reserves from the commercial banking system during this period of time, the commercial banking system had enough "excess" reserves in the system to allow the money stock to continue to increase...and to allow the real economy to grow at relatively decent rates.

This has great significance for the future.

The Trump administration has focused on getting the Federal Reserve to reduce its policy rate of interest by a relatively excessive amount.

Chairman Powell and the Board of Governors of the Fed have not been willing to do that.

Chairman Powell and the Board have focused upon a "quantity," the amount of securities in the portfolios of the commercial banking system...and not upon a price...the effective Federal Funds rate.

In doing so, Mr. Powell and the Fed have done a really good job in keeping the U.S. economy functioning...and growing.

It is the intent of Mr. Powell and the Federal Reserve leadership to continue this path.

And, after more than three and one-half years of "quantitative tightening," Mr. Powell and the Fed are ready to move on.

Exactly what they plan to move on to is not known, but the direction will surely be to move to a stance of modest quantitative easing.

From what we can see from the Fed's balance sheet, the banking system has plenty of "excess" reserves around to support further monetary growth.

Right now, on November 12, 2025, the Federal Reserve records $2.9 trillion of excess reserves in the commercial banking system.

The U.S. commercial banking system is very, very liquid!

The U.S. commercial banking system could go on and let the M2 money stock continue to grow by at least 4.0 percent for an extended period of time.

And what does this mean about economic growth?

Well, I would say that the chart for the economic growth of the U.S. economy could look pretty much like the above chart for another two years or so.

That is, I believe that the U.S. economy could extend its present growth pattern out until the end of the Trump administration, with the Federal Reserve doing very little else to stimulate the economy.

Yes, I believe that the commercial banking system has sufficient reserves right now to underwrite the continued growth in the M2 money stock so as to keep the economy moving in a 2.0 percent to 3.0 percent range until Trump goes out of office.

That is if there is not a lot of messing around with the system during this period of time.

Of course, this picture could be easily destroyed if it is "managed" too much.

But that, again, is another story...which we must deal with in the future.

Unfortunately, I don't see the Trump administration standing back and doing nothing about this picture.

data.title: Nvidia: Being Greedy Before Earnings While Others Hesitate
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) is set to release its FQ3 2026 earnings next week (November 19th), and the setup to buy before earnings looks too good to ignore. The recent pullback washed out all the hype around the stock, and I would always prefer fear over greed before the upcoming earnings release. A selloff before earnings likely means that investors are becoming concerned about Nvidia's ability to outperform further. However, the analysis of recent developments suggests that there is no fundamental basis for all these fears, as the AI spending remains aggressive. The stock is way below the consensus target price, which indicates that the stock is substantially undervalued. This assertion is supported by the forward P/E ratio analysis. Even given the month-to-date NVDA share price weakness, the stock is still ahead of the broader market since my previous Strong Buy recommendation, which I reiterate.

NVDA Q3 earnings preview

In the previous NVDA coverage, I calculated the target price for NVDA to be around $204. The stock broke through the psychological level of $200 in late October, but it dropped below $190 as the month-to-date performance was weak, mostly due to the overall technology sector's weakness as investors absorbed Q3 earnings of cloud giants with cautiousness. Since NVDA is a high-beta stock, it plunged deeper than the sector and the broader market.

However, I want to explain why the other companies' Q3 earnings were actually positive for Nvidia. All of them decided to increase their AI infrastructure budgets both for calendar 2025 and 2026, meaning that the data center spending spree is not fading. Of course, investors of these companies (like Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Google (GOOGL) (GOOG)) got a little bit spooked by these aggressive plans because payback periods are still highly uncertain. But it relates only to investors of the companies that are spending big on data centers. However, Nvidia does not invest in all these large data centers worth billions of dollars but actually earns by selling essential GPUs for them. Therefore, Nvidia should have rallied on the news regarding increased AI infrastructure spending instead of dipping.

Moreover, Nvidia's competitors in the data center business, like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Intel (INTC), already released their Q3 earnings, and both of them delivered strong performance with the management's big optimism for the expanding total addressable market, or TAM, for the entire semiconductor industry. AMD's CEO Lisa Su said that the data center TAM size will hit $1 trillion in just a few years. Nvidia is miles ahead in terms of data center revenue compared to AMD and Intel, meaning that it has the largest market share and is firmly positioned to become the biggest beneficiary of the rapidly expanding TAM.

There is also one more confirmation that demand from data centers keeps soaring, which recently came from the memory chips domain. It was announced just today that Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) has raised its data center memory chip prices by a staggering 60% due to supply shortage. The magnitude of this increase is just enormous, and it underscores how big the supply from data centers is. We could have thought that it is just some Samsung-specific strength, but it doesn't seem so, as Micron (MU) is also set to raise prices due to the DRAM shortage.

The fact that demand for chips from data centers remains strong is also confirmed by recent earnings revisions from Wall Street. Revenue revisions were all green over the last month, suggesting that prominent analysts tend to agree with the assertion that AI tailwinds are not decelerating. Since we see that memory chips are raising prices, it is also highly likely that Nvidia will also be able to exercise pricing power while the demand soars. Therefore, it is no surprise that EPS revisions were also all positive over the last month.

Nvidia is also extremely good in terms of delivering positive earnings surprises, with eleven consecutive quarters with both revenue and EPS beats. With strong AI tailwinds and Nvidia's historically strong performance during earnings seasons, I think that it is reasonable to reiterate my bullish outlook.

Last but not least, the 14-day RSI indicator. I follow it closely before earnings because it indicates the market's sentiment ahead of this crucial catalyst. I think the reason why NVDA did not quite fly after the Q2 earnings release, despite blowout earnings, might be the extent of overexcitement—the RSI indicator was above 60 the day before the Q2 earnings release. This time, the sentiment is much more neutral, as the RSI stands at around 46. With lower expectations from the public, it will be much easier for Nvidia to impress investors.

Valuation analysis

The forward P/E ratio setup is extremely bullish as the rapid multiple contraction is expected. Nvidia's P/E is expected to shrink by around three times between FY2025 actual and FY2029 estimate, even though Wall Street analysts forecast the EPS growth rate to decline significantly every year. For the largest AI beneficiary among the entire tech sector, such a rapid P/E ratio contraction suggests notable undervaluation.

The estimated FY2026 forward P/E ratio of around 41 is significantly lower than Nvidia's historical average of circa 46. On average, Wall Street analysts forecast the FY2027 (next fiscal year) full-year EPS to be $6.72. I rely on this forecast because the sample looks representative, as this forecast is an average opinion of more than fifty Wall Street analysts. Moreover, we see that AI tailwinds are still strong, and big tech are accelerating their data center spending, which has a direct positive effect on Nvidia's EPS growth stability.

If we multiply Nvidia's historical average P/E by the FY2027 EPS forecast, we can see that the fair value per share is $309.5. This is my target price for the next twelve months, which means that the stock is massively undervalued. And even if we multiply the historical average P/E of 46 by this fiscal year's EPS forecast ($4.54), we get a fair value of around $209. It means that even an ultra-conservative approach suggests double-digit upside potential.

Another evidence suggesting that NVDA is substantially undervalued before the upcoming earnings release is the fact that Wall Street's average target price is around $232. With that being said, Nvidia is around 24% undervalued compared to the consensus target price.

Mitigating factors

While I like NVDA's pullback before earnings because it made the stock even more undervalued, let's not forget that the public's cautious sentiment might be a serious negative catalyst even for Nvidia. I think that all Nvidia bulls remember very well how the stock plunged by more than 20% in January 2025 just because of the market's fears after the Chinese DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) large language model ("LLM") release. And so NVDA did during the Liberation Day panic, which means that the stock is not bulletproof against the broader market's sell-off. The broader market's relatively weak performance so far in November 2025 does not look like a panic, but nobody knows exactly whether it is just a healthy pullback before the rally continues or investors believe that we achieved peak valuations.

On the one hand, the S&P 500 performance from the late October peak does not look that bad with just a 2.2% drop. We haven't seen any big one-day dips like we evidenced during DeepSeek or Liberation Day panics. On the other hand, it might be just the beginning, as the market switched to the "Extreme Fear" mode quite swiftly. Anyway, the current situation creates additional uncertainty for investors, which they hate. And many people might choose sitting on the fence at the moment, which is a potential notable share price headwind for NVDA.

Conclusion

I can't stop buying Nvidia before its Q3 earnings release because we are highly likely to see another robust quarter while the stock looks notably undervalued. Therefore, my take is that NVDA remains a Strong Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The U.S. Economic Paradox: GDP Is Growing, But Your Finances Are Getting Worse
data.description:
Something doesn't add up

The GDP is the most used economic indicator when it comes to evaluating the financial health of a specific country. Typically, periods where GDP experienced a contraction were associated with a severe recession; on the other hand, strong GDP growth is usually a bullish sign for the economy and the underlying stock market. It's as simple as that.

If we look at GDPNow published by the Atlanta Fed, the expectations for Q3 US real GDP point to an impressive 4% expansion, a figure that is pretty high for such a mature economy. It seems that everything is going great and the US is living a new growth phase driven by AI. However, as I stated in a previous article, some data are showing us a pretty different situation: consumer sentiment is close to 2008 levels, and finding a new job is way harder than a couple of years ago.

How is it possible that real GDP is growing fast but consumers feel depressed? A financial paradox is underway, and I believe it lies in the way the real GDP is calculated. In particular, I think there are two components that are making its outcome misleading:

  • AI CapEx
  • Consumer heterogeneity

Let’s start with the first one.

AI CapEx is supporting economic growth

To keep growing, the US economy relies heavily on households spending on goods and services. To be precise, we are talking about 68.80% of the 2024 GDP. In other words, more than 2/3 of the US GDP depends on how much households are about to spend.

According to Fitch Ratings, US consumer spending is deteriorating significantly. In Q1 2025, the growth rate dropped to 0.50%, and in Q2 2025, it was 1.40%, way below the 3.70% and 4% achieved in Q3 2024 and Q4 2024, respectively. So, in light of these data, we should expect an underwhelming 2025 real GDP growth, right? Well, not exactly.

The real GDP is growing pretty fast, and this chart doesn’t take into account the strong growth of Q3 2025 (probably about 4%). The only flaw was recorded in Q1 2025, but that data was mainly influenced by record imports due to tariff changes, a kind of one-off negative factor that is already behind us.

The reason why the US real GDP is growing way faster than US household spending depends on the unprecedented investments in the AI race. The US hyperscalers are investing like never before, and this is having a major impact on the entire economy.

Over the last 3 years, their CapEx increased by triple digits, and in most cases outpaced the Cash from Operations growth.

Over the last 12 months, they invested $357 billion, and this amount is expected to be higher in 2026. They are investing so much that their financial position is worsening, as you can see from the rising net financial debt.

In 2020, most of them had way more cash than debt; now just Alphabet has a positive net cash. This doesn’t mean that US Big Tech is facing a debt sustainability problem (that would be absurd), but it shows us how much they are investing to lead the AI revolution. They are willing to get to extreme levels of CapEx, even if this means getting more debt. Meta is a recent example of that, as it is planning to issue $30 billion worth of bonds despite generating a Cash from Operations of >$100 billion over the last 12 months. But how is the AI CapEx impacting real GDP?

According to Harvard professor Jason Furman, even though investment in information processing equipment & software is just 4% of GDP, it accounted for 92% of the GDP growth during H1 2025. This means that, without this component, the actual GDP growth would have been just 0.10% during H1 2025. Almost no growth, but Jason Furman pointed out that this estimate lacks the opportunity cost:

Absent the AI boom we would probably have lower interest rates [and] electricity prices, thus some additional growth in other sectors. In very rough terms that could maybe make up about half of what we got from the AI boom.

Therefore, we can’t just take off the table the AI CapEx and state that the economy is going nowhere. Without AI, companies would have invested in other sectors, still leading to GDP expansion. However, we can reasonably state that US companies wouldn’t have invested so much in a different context, so these unprecedented investments in AI are taking up a crucial role for the US economy.

As of now, in terms of GDP growth, investments in AI data centers are more important than household spending. On one hand, this is great because it is offsetting the underwhelming household spending, but on the other hand, I wonder how long this expansion phase can last. US hyperscalers can still afford to invest hundreds of billions of dollars, but there will be a moment when they will slow down their enthusiasm. Once the AI infrastructure has been built, the GDP growth loses one of its biggest engines.

The consumer heterogeneity is expanding

If you are wondering why the GDP is growing fast, but your spending capacity remains the same or even deteriorates, the answer lies in the consumer heterogeneity.

In the previous paragraph I showed you that the US household spending growth rate dropped considerably compared to last year, but it is still solid (1.40% in Q2 2025). What I didn’t tell you is that much of this growth comes from a strict number of households.

According to a Moody’s analysis, since the pandemic, the US spending growth stemmed mainly from the wealthiest households. If you are reading this article, there is a high probability that you belong to the bottom 80%, a large group of households whose spending barely keep up with inflation.

Looking at the data, it’s not a mystery why most Americans feel like the economy isn’t working for them. For those in the bottom 80% of the income distribution, those making less than approximately $175,000 a year, their spending has simply kept pace with inflation since the pandemic.

The 20% of households that make more have done much better, and those in the top 3.3% of the distribution have done much, much, much better.

Moody’s chief economist Mark Zandi

In other words, US households’ consumption is not growing because everyone is spending more. It is increasing because the wealthiest households spending has more than offset the weakened position of the bottom 80% of households (the majority of the US population).

At this point, you might wonder why this is happening, and the stock market performance plays a major role.

The top 0.10% owns assets worth $23.56 trillion, more than 2x the bottom 50%. The 90%-99% group owns assets worth $64.89 trillion, more than 6x the bottom 50%. A huge gap that is widening over time, and if we look at the asset’s composition of every group, we understand why.

As you can see, the richer you are, the more equities make up most of your net worth. That’s a huge difference compared to the bottom 50%, where equities are just a small fraction of their wealth (Real estate takes up 50% of it).

This divergence explains a lot.

Since the stock market is soaring thanks to the AI trend, the rich got even richer, and the gap will widen as long as the stock market keeps recording new all-time highs. Even though you don’t sell your stocks, when you see huge unrealized gains in your portfolio, you tend to spend more. Plus, with a securities-based line of credit (SBLOC), you can even get ample liquidity using your portfolio as a collateral.

In such a strong bullish phase, I am not surprised that the wealthiest families are spending more. At the same time, the bottom 50% can’t really see the benefits of a roaring stock market, as their equity position is not large enough.

When the stock market experiences a drop of 15-20%, this virtuous circle could be interrupted, and this might have significant consequences on GDP growth. If even the wealthiest families struggle, there are no more safety nets for US consumer spending.

Conclusion

The US economy is experiencing solid real GDP growth, but as we have seen, the devil is in the details. Consumer spending growth is slowing down, and it is supported by a small percentage of US households; AI spending is compensating for the consumer spending deterioration, but it can’t last forever. There will be a point where the US hyperscalers can no longer spend hundreds of billions of dollars, or they just won’t need to anymore: the current CapEx levels are extreme.

Therefore, if we take a closer look at the GDP composition, the US economy doesn’t seem so thriving. Most of the US households don’t feel in a better financial position than a few years ago, even though the stock market suggests otherwise.

data.title: PayPal: Growth Engine With Deteriorating Bottom-Lines - Reversal In Progress
data.description:
I previously covered PayPal Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:PYPL) in August 2025, discussing how its ongoing turnaround had been met with much skepticism, as observed in the steep sell-off after the lumpy FQ2'25 cash flow performance.

Given the double-beat FQ2'25 performance, the raised FY2025 top/bottom-line guidance, the robust active account growth for six consecutive quarters, and the ongoing share retirement, I had believed that the stock might offer a double-digit upside potential from prior levels - resulting in my reiterated Buy rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss how PYPL's ongoing focus on fintech innovation has delivered renewed growth across Venmo and BNPL, albeit triggering impacted profit margins - with it likely contributing to the stock's recent underperformance against its high growth, profitable fintech peers.

On the other hand, assuming that the new management is able to drive the further acceleration in its top-lines and the future expansion in its profit margins, I reckon that the market may eventually re-rate the stock nearer to its historical trends and its fintech peers - with the rich upside potential triggering my reiterated Buy rating here.

PYPL Finally Delivers New Growth

PYPL 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Buy article, PYPL has mostly traded sideways against the wider market gain at single digits, while underperforming that of its high growth fintech peers by varying degrees.

It is apparent that the legacy fintech company continues to face great competition as a digital wallet player, as more players/start-ups, Big Tech companies, and even, banks enter the fray.

1. Accelerating Top-Line Growth Through New Innovations

These reasons may also be why PYPL's new management team has attempted to drive growth and adoption through numerous fintech innovations, including Agentic Commerce Offerings, PayPal Ads, Pay with Crypto (including stablecoin), and Venmo Debit/Credit cards, amongst others.

Growing Innovations Trigger Growth Shoots

Some of these efforts have been reflected in PYPL's growing Venmo Total Payment Volume [TPV] by +12% YoY and Payment Service Provider [PSP] & Other Consumer segment by +11% YoY, despite the tougher YoY comparison from +8% YoY and +7% YoY, respectively.

Particularly, the growing adoption of PayPal consumer accounts/Venmo has already triggered the increased Monthly Active Accounts [MAA] to 227M (+0.4% QoQ/+2.2% YoY) and the higher number of payment transactions (ex PSP) of 4.03B (+2.2% QoQ/+7% YoY), with it reflecting a growing mindshare in the crowded fintech space.

These numbers are not surprising indeed, since PYPL has sought to deepen consumer engagement in the Venmo space, which has contributed to Venmo's growing MAA to 66M (+7% YoY) and revenue growth by +20% YoY.

A similar trend may be observed in its PayPal/Venmo debit card, where "PayPal debit card actives transact ~6X more and generate ~3X ARPA compared to checkout-only actives," along with the BNPL offerings, where both TPV and MAA grows by +20% YoY.

These reasons are also why I am of the opinion that PYPL remains an extremely relevant fintech player despite the intensifying competition, as the new management delivers accelerated growth across its branded channels (credit/debit and branded checkout), Venmo, and P2P.

2. PYPL's Focus On Innovation Comes With A Bottom-Line Cost

Impacted Profit Margins

On the other hand, readers need not look far to understand why PYPL's stock prices continue to underperform, with the aggressive fintech launches and consumer acquisition strategy already triggering the mixed adj operating margins of 18.6% in FQ3'25 (-1.2 points QoQ/-0.2 YoY/-4.6 from FY2019 levels of 23.2%).

While these efforts have notably driven its top-line growth to $8.41B (+1.5% QoQ/+7.2% YoY), it goes without saying that the elevated operational/tech development expenses have triggered the fintech's underwhelming bottom-line performances.

This is especially since PYPL's adj net income performance has been relatively lumpy at $1.28B (-6.5% QoQ/+4.9% YoY), with the richer adj EPS performance at $1.34 (-4.2% QoQ/+11.6% YoY) partly attributed to the lower share count by -6.2% YoY.

A similar trend may also be observed in the fintech's impacted Transaction Margin performance at 46% (-0.4 points QoQ/-0.6 YoY/-7.8 from FQ4'19 levels of 53.8%), with the slower Transaction Margin $ growth by +6% YoY further negated by the higher volume-based transaction expenses growth by +8% YoY.

Pending a further acceleration in its top-lines and/or a moderation in its operating expenses, I reckon that PYPL's profit margins may underwhelm in the near-term, with it perhaps explaining why the stock continues to trade sideways thus far.

Discounted Valuations Trigger Buy Opportunity

The Consensus Forward Estimates

For now, PYPL has already twice raised the FY2025 transaction margin to $15.5B at the midpoint (+5.4% YoY) and adj EPS to $5.37 (+15.4% YoY) while building upon the FY2024 levels of +7.2% YoY and -8.8% YoY, respectively.

These perhaps explain why the consensus have been raising their forward estimates, with the legacy fintech company expected to report an accelerated bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +12.6% through FY2027, expanded compared to the 5Y historical growth profile at +8.4%.

Despite the lumpy adj free cash flow generation of $2.27B in FQ3'25 (+47.4% YoY) and $4.3B on a YTD basis (-5% YoY), PYPL has announced an unexpected dividend program in the recent earnings call, with an initial quarterly payment of $0.14.

Most importantly, the management has highlighted how the dividend payouts may grow inline with its adj net income profitability, based on "a targeted payout ratio of 10% of non-GAAP net income," with it highlighting how patient investors may be rewarded during the fintech's ongoing turnaround to profitable growth.

While current payouts imply a mere forward dividend yield of 0.8%, I am already encouraged by PYPL's robust shareholder return program indeed, with it building upon the -19.1% of its float retired since FY2019.

PYPL Valuations

As a result of the excellent use of its cash flow and the management's confidence in delivering profitable, growth cadence through new innovations, I am of the opinion that PYPL's discounted FWD P/E non-GAAP valuation of 12.39x is extremely cheap against the 1Y mean of 14.63x, the 5Y mean of 25.53x, and its peer group P/E mean of ~33x.

A similar conclusion may also be observed in PYPL's estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.98x (based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuation of 12.39x and the consensus 3Y adj EPS growth at a CAGR of +12.6%), compared to the 5Y mean of 1.63x, the sector median of 1.08x, and its profitable fintech peers, including:

  • Mastercard (MA) at 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.24x (using the same methodology),
  • Visa (V) at 2.14x,
  • SoFi Technologies (SOFI) at 1.09x,
  • Block (XYZ) at 2.77x,
  • Affirm (AFRM) at 0.63x, and
  • Upstart (UPST) at 0.69x,

with it apparent that the former continues to offer a deep value investment thesis for those with a longer investing trajectory.

Upgrading In Valuations May Be A Matter Of Time

PYPL 8Y Stock Price

Otherwise, PYPL's ongoing sideways trading since the April 2025 recovery continues to highlight its lack of bullish support, as similarly observed in the moderated stock price gains despite the recent OpenAI partnership announcement.

PYPL Technical Indicators

PYPL's technical indicators across the reasonable RSIs and moderating trading volumes continue to point at its compelling investment thesis, albeit with it remaining to be seen when the stock may break out from current levels similar to historical trends between 2017 - H1'19 and Q2'20 - Q3'21, or continue trading sideways at the $60s ranges as it has since early 2022.

These underscore why the stock remains an unappreciated underdog in the fintech world despite the improving monetization trends, the growing active accounts, and the twice raised FY2025 guidance, as similarly observed in the discounted valuations compared to its peers.

Based on PYPL's raised FY2025 adj EPS guidance to $5.27 at the midpoint (+15.4% YoY) and the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuation of 12.39x (discounted compared to the peer group P/E mean of ~33x), the stock is still trading near to my base-case fair value estimates of $65.20.

Based on the consensus raised FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $6.63, there remains an excellent upside potential of +21.8% to my base-case long-term price target of $82.10 as well.

Moving forward, I am of the opinion that PYPL may be eventually upgraded nearer to its historical levels and/or its fintech peers, as the new management team delivers green shoots in its long-term growth prospects as discussed above.

A similar trend has been previously observed in another underdog, Alphabet (GOOG), based on the deeply discounted valuations at a 3Y P/E mean of ~20x between Q2'22 and Q2'25, compared to its Magnificent 7 peers at a P/E mean of ~42x over the same time period.

Well, this is before the much needed clarity from the recent anti-trust ruling, which triggers the sudden reversal in market sentiments and the notable upgrading in GOOG's FWD P/E to ~27x by the time of writing.

Assuming a similar upgrading cadence for PYPL upon the acceleration of its top-line growth and the expansion of its profit margins, readers may look forward to an eventual re-rating in its P/E valuations to its 5Y P/E mean of ~25x, which may consequently trigger a more than doubling upside potential to my bull-case long-term price target of $165.70.

These reasons are also why I am of the opinion that the legacy fintech stock remains a compelling deep-value play for patient investors, since its upcoming (and speculative) reversal are likely to trigger rich capital appreciation prospects.

I am maintaining my Buy rating for the PYPL stock here.

data.title: Telecom Argentina: Caught Between Macro Hope And Fundamental Reality
data.description:
Telecom Argentina S.A. (TEO) has a complex investment thesis, marked by being the main telecom company in a country that is recovering from a scenario of hyperinflation, strong exchange rate volatility, and a history of regulatory interventions that distort traditional profitability metrics.

Both Telecom Argentina and other local equities have been largely influenced by the unfolding political narrative, as President Javier Milei's libertarian agenda moves forward with the aim of containing inflation and re-establishing a sustainable economy in the country for private enterprise.

On the fundamentals side, Telecom Argentina has made positive progress as inflation has cooled, doing its "homework" by streamlining operations and deleveraging. And for that, it deserves credit.

With a less "noisy" and potentially much less "risky" political scenario following the victory of Javier Milei's party in the midterm elections, TEO has re-rated vigorously since the beginning of November.

At the same time, with valuation metrics proving more "rational" than they should be at the beginning of 2025, potentially catching up with fundamentals in the foreseeable future, I believe there is less room for bearishness in the thesis of TEO going further.

A Rapid Recap

Following on from my coverage of Telecom Argentina, in my last article, I emphasized that the company was fundamentally in a stronger position, mainly driven by a better macro scenario. However, the bearish rating was sustained purely due to valuation disconnects, in my perception.

Considering that Argentine equities have broadly performed, driven by advances in the country's political scene, much of TEO's recent performance is linked to the advances of Javier Milei's policies in the country's presidency.

In a nutshell, Milei and his economic team have defied skeptics by implementing austerity policies to combat hyperinflation, cutting public spending at its roots, bringing Argentina from hyperinflation of ~211% and a deficit of ~5% of GDP at the end of 2023 to current inflation of 43% per year (with prospects of converting to 28% by the end of this year), and also registering a primary surplus in the first four years of his mandate-something unheard of since 2008.

If from Milei's election at the end of November 2023 until January of this year, the TEO jumped by more than 160%, the year 2025 proved to be a troubled one, until October at least, with the Milei administration facing its most challenging time since then.

As well as 2025 being a structural phase of the Milei administration's reforms, which in theory are more politically costly and depend more on Congress, this was a more difficult stage than 2024, which was marked by faster measures such as abrupt spending cuts.

What's more, Argentine society entered the "adjustment fatigue" phase that year, making protests and social tensions more frequent—including greater union and student participation—and Milei's approval ratings fell to record lows. There was also a return to the debate on the exchange rate and the monetary regime, where the original plan of exchange rate unification, liberalization of the foreign exchange market, and eventual dollarization did not proceed as scripted. The delay in the real exchange rate, the pace of exchange rate convergence, and the lack of consensus on dollarization brought noise in 2025, especially since exchange rate and monetary policy define inflation expectations and directly influence local equities like TEO.

To make matters worse, the potential accusation of a corruption scandal against important members of his government, and the defeat in the Buenos Aires provincial election (where the country's largest electorate is concentrated) at the beginning of September, was read as a bad omen by the market. The midterm congressional elections that took place in late October, determined halfway through the presidential term, would be crucial for Milei and his party to achieve the governability needed to pursue the libertarian macroeconomic reform agenda.

The defeat in the province of Buenos Aires signaled fears that Milei's party would not win a majority in Congress. This affected Argentine equities so much that the ARGT ETF (which covers the main U.S. equities) and the TEO hit their lows throughout the year.

The big relief came in late October with the victory of Milei's party in the midterms, signaling great confidence that governability would be guaranteed, and that there is room to project perhaps a re-election of Milei. As a result, double-digit gains in single trading sessions for most of the Argentine equities, especially Telecom Argentina.

TEO's Macro Relief Is Real, But So Are Its Volume and FX Headwinds

If macroeconomic risk was the main driver of pressure on TEO's performance, the relief caused by the victory of Milei's party in the midterm elections brought an extremely welcome decompression to the thesis. After all, much of the improvement in Telecom Argentina's financial indicators—and those of other equities in the country—has essentially been a reflection of cooling inflation, a greater flow of foreign investors into the country, and GDP returning to healthy growth.

Specifically in the case of Telecom Argentina, a brief parenthesis to be opened is that in February the company incorporated Telecomunicaciones Móviles Argentina S.A., (aka TMA), an arm responsible for mobile services, symbolizing an internal corporate reorganization. Under the premise of unifying synergies, Telecom Argentina incorporated TMA by taking on US$126 million in debt and a further US$1.1 billion to be repaid via two loans.

That said, the consolidated results for 9M '25 include seven months of TMA contribution, which distorts the comparative basis with 2024. And when looking at the quarterly release and referring to the nine months so far this year, TEO's consolidated revenues came in 50.7% higher YoY, but up to +4% YoY if you exclude TMA.

Of course, 4% nominal growth in a country with 43% inflation per year is very weak, and would support, in theory, that price pass-through remains inefficient even with the significant improvement in inflation over the last two years.

However, when we look at real ARPU, this contradicts the narrative of loss of pricing power.

TEO's mobile ARPU grew 5% in 9M25, while broadband and pay TV grew 10% and 3%, respectively. If, on the one hand, there is pricing power in ARPU, but TEO is unable to convert this into revenue growth, it means that the problem lies in volume, showing that the company seems to be in a stage of growing "per customer" but shrinking in "number of customers."

This is largely reinforced by the fact that in Mobile, TEO lost 6.4% YoY in prepaid and lost 2.8% YoY in postpaid, an indication that lines were lost in both segments. Even if combined growth plus prepaid falling -3.9% YoY and postpaid growing 0.8% YoY, this means that without TMA, core would have had a much stronger drop in base.

Having said these clearly bearish points on the top line, there are positive points to be noted, especially with regard to the actual operational recovery. The OIBDA margin in 9M '25 came in at 30.5% (up 1.7 p.p. YoY), and 31.4% in 3Q25 (up 6.8 p.p. YoY), excluding TMA. This is undoubtedly very strong for TEO, showing cost discipline and mix improvement, where the company has done what it can within its reach.

In addition, CapEx representing 15.1% of revenue in 9M '25, remained under control, re-emphasizing discipline and reducing cash pressure. Free cash flow ended up at US$531 in LTM 9M '25, up from US$404 million in the whole of fiscal 2024, and a cash position of US$498 million.

Perhaps the most bullish point has been the sharp drop in leverage, from 3.2x in 2023 to stand at 1.9x in Q3 '25 for the second consecutive quarter. Even with weak revenue, margin expansion, debt reduction in USD (via inflation), and the dilution of FX impact with lower EBITDA, it implied a relief on the default risk or equity dilution aspect-practically putting it off the table.

The caveat here is that the fall in FX continues to impact Telecom Argentina's net profit and dilute equity. So much so that the 9M '25 net loss (P$272.5 billion) is mainly due to foreign exchange losses. The devaluation of the ARS vs. the USD, at the time of writing, already amounts to 26.5%-and over 94.3% in the last five years.

Understanding TEO's Re-Rating

Returning to valuations, which is my biggest point of skepticism in the thesis, I believe that this remains a "gray area" of the thesis, as Argentina's macroeconomic environment tends to greatly distort profitability figures. In my previous coverage, I had determined through a DCF model using assumptions based on the market consensus top line that the company would be fairly priced at ~$5 per share ADR.

Until mid-October, my price target seemed to be on track, with TEO reaching nearly $6.50 at the height of skepticism due to the events preceding the midterm elections in Argentina.

But of course, estimating cash flows and discounting them to present value is highly speculative, and my price target proved to be very conservative given TEO's recent performance—it is trading at $13.30 at the moment I write this.

However, considering EBITDA numbers in USD, eight months ago, when I had a bearish view on valuations, TEO was trading at an EV/EBITDA of 6x to 7x, while Latin American peers such as Brazil's Telefônica Brasil (VIV) and TIM S.A. (TIMB), and Mexico's América Móvil (AMX) were trading between 3x and 4x, all in a much healthier macroeconomic scenario than Argentina's.

And before the midterm elections, TEO was trading at 3.5x EBITDA, a level that I considered more reasonable for the company at the time, given its operational challenges. Now, all four major Latin American telephone companies are trading at virtually the same levels.

But does TEO deserve to trade at valuation multiples close to its peers in Latin America, which arguably have more solid fundamentals and operate in a much more favorable macro environment?

I still don't think so. I believe that the recent re-rating of TEO's multiple was not because the company got better, but because Argentina got "less worse."
