The key point is that a "less worse" Argentina means a much better TEO in the future. This made me rethink the way I look at TEO's valuations and consider the premium for a "regime change" in Argentina. In other words, I think it is more worthwhile to look at TEO as a "country risk equity" rather than solely on profit-driven valuations. After all, if Argentina manages to converge its annual inflation to 14% annually by the end of 2026, as domestic investment banks estimate, I believe that the value of the equity could materially increase. And arguably, if this materializes, TEO's fundamentals will simultaneously reach levels comparable to its continental peers—if not surpass them.

Final Remarks

As much as there are points of skepticism about Telecom Argentina's fundamentals and questions about valuations being stretched, the correction since the peak at the beginning of the year arguably brings more plausible multiples. I believe that the post-midterm elections re-rating brings back the narrative of being priced above the fundamentals in the thesis, although still with much more moderation than was seen in early 2025.

However, I would say that Telecom Argentina continues to improve its fundamentals as the Argentine economy recovers, especially foreseeing a much milder inflationary scenario for 2025 and potentially 2026.

Considering that the market works under expectations, today I see the valuation premium at ~5x EV/EBITDA as more "fitting" to the thesis, and a bearish approach seems too stubborn to be sustained. The risk perception of the political narrative under Argentine equities has materially dissipated, and a re-rating is in order. However, compared to continental peers—which I think have much stronger fundamentals and a much milder macro scenario—I believe Telecom Argentina is lagging.

Although I remain bullish on the recovery of the Argentine economy and Argentine equities in general, it seems risky to stick with solely TEO for now, and so a Hold rating here seems appropriate in my view.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Seeking 15%+ Yields? Here Are 2 BDCs I'm Buying
data.description:
My closest followers have most likely noticed that when it comes to BDCs, I have become more bullish than earlier in the year before the sector entered into a free-fall.

However, as I laid it out in my recent piece on the BDC environment - The BDC Bargain Bell Is Ringing - there are still some unpleasant headwinds remaining such as lower base rates, tight base dividend coverage levels and generally exhausted balance sheets.

To navigate these issues and, importantly, to capitalize on the tailwinds from the increased M&A activity, I think that investors have to be very selective here. Namely, high-quality equity investment exposures, strong balance sheets with ample debt capacities and robust underwriting standards are key for success.

Having said that, while this is my overarching core strategy through which I expect to capture durable current income streams (without the expense of NAV loss), I have remained open to venturing in some tactical plays.

By a tactical play I mean BDCs, which offer very compelling price to value ratio. The value might be much lower (NAV of a lower quality) than for my core holdings, but as long as the price is even lower (deeply below NAV), I would for sure consider such investments in my portfolio. Granted, the allocation to them would be much lower than for core holdings; just to balance the risks and avoid potential income volatility.

In the article below, I would like to elaborate on two tactical BDCs, which I have included in my portfolio even though they lack the key characteristics that I typically seek in BDCs for sustainable income extraction.

Pick #1 - FS KKR Capital

The first one is FS KKR Capital (FSK). Here I have to provide a bit of a history.

My first article on FSK was issued in December 2023, where I compared FSK (the second-largest BDC) with Ares Capital (ARCC) (the largest BDC). I decided to go with FSK mainly because of much steeper discount - at that time around 20%. After a year of holding period, in January, 2025, I decided to harvest my profits, locking in ~27% in total returns. From that moment until I issued my recent piece on FSK, the BDC contracted by almost 30%.

Now, if we measured the performance of my FSK holding now, we would notice a positive result of ~8.8%, which is significantly above the BDC (BIZD) index.

I think that it would be fair to say that my FSK trades from the timing perspective have been quite acceptable.

The question is whether it is perhaps a good moment to quickly lock in the high single digit return and reallocate the capital into some other, higher quality BDCs.

Well, I do not think so. Here is why.

FSK continues to trade at a P/NAV of 0.70x, which indicates a discount of 30%. It is a huge number and also a meaningful gap from the sector average discount of 15%. What this implies is that the market is not buying FSK's NAV figure. In other words, the expectation is that going forward it will drop by converging closer to the current price. So, let's unpack the NAV.

In recent Q3, 2025 figures, FSK reported an increase in NAV per share by 2.7% on quarter-to-quarter basis. The reason for that was simple - net unrealized and realized gain component, which benefited from FSK's non-accrual restructuring and two profitable investment exits. Plus, there was another positive restructuring event right after Q3, which resulted in a further reduction in non-accrual rate and, of course, in an uptick of NAV per share. The non-accrual rate has declined to 1.9% of a FV basis (considering the post Q3 restructured loan). In this context, we have to also appreciate the fact that in Q3 FSK distributed $0.70 per share of dividend of which $0.06 per share was a supplemental. Based on the current share price the $0.70 per share payout translates to ~4.4% yield (in one quarter alone). So, even with this kind of aggressive payout, FSK managed to boost its NAV.

Speaking of non-accruals, I think that it would be fair to say that exactly this part of FSK's business is what worries the market most. In other words, the weak non-accrual history creates concerns about unfavorable surprises in the future. I have three things to say here:

  1. The past couple of quarters have clearly indicated that the trend in non-accruals is improved. There have still been some spikes (such as in Q2, 2025), the overarching direction of travel remains positive. This is logical considering the fact that most of the non-accruals over the past years have really stemmed from the Corporate Capital Trust side, which was merged in FSK back in 2019. We are now in the very late innings of a completely recycled loan portfolio that is underpinned by FSK underwritten loans.
  2. The underlying portfolio investment companies are in good shape as, for example, in Q3 their weighted average EBITDA generation increased by 4% on a year-over-year basis.
  3. The fact that base rates are lower and will likely continue to fall, FSK portfolio cash flows should benefit.

What could drive down NAV is the aggressive distributions. However, it has been already communicated by the management that going forward the base dividend will be at $0.45 per share and supported by complementary distributions in order to extract full NII per share out of the system. For example, for Q1, 2026, the expectation is to pay out $0.55 per share, which based on current share price leads to just below 15% yield. Plus, if we assume that Q3 NII per share remains in place at $0.57, the dividend story looks pretty strong.

Now, do I believe that FSK will manage to keep NII per share of $0.57? No. It will most likely go down with further base rate reductions and unfavorable spread convergence (i.e., current portfolio high spread investments being slowly but surely replaced with investments that carry market-level spreads).

Do I believe that FSK's NII per share will drop below $0.45 (by more than 22%)? Absolutely not. Even if we assume a very pessimistic scenario of $0.45 per share distributions, we would still be getting an enticing yield of ~11.5%

In my view, FSK is relatively stable NAV and high yield play with significant price upside due to abnormal discount.

Pick #2 - CION Investment

CION Investment Corporation (CION) is my second tactical BDC investment, which currently yields 14.9% and trades at a P/NAV of 0.66x (i.e., a discount of 34%). I have been long CION since August, 2024, where the trade has resulted in flat total returns. However, in the past couple of months, the share price has not only stabilized, but also shown some first signs of recovery. In last month specifically - which includes Q3 report - CION is up by ~8%, which is definitely one of the best performance in the sector.

Again, just as in the case of FSK, a 30%+ discount means that the market does not think that the current NAV figure is reflective of long-term value.

Obviously, CION has above average risk, no questions about that. But, in my view, the distance between price and value is too long. Namely, I am not arguing that CION should trade at NAV, but at least significantly closer to it than what the share price indicates now. I will explain.

The key driver for CION's discount is considerable payment-in-kind streams. The PIK explains the lion's share of total income, whereas the average level for BDCs is around 10%. The problem with PIK is that the income is non-cash and instead comes in the form of increased outstanding loan principal (investment value for CION). Usually, such structures are offered to struggling businesses to manage their cash flow issues. So, I fully understand why the market has assigned notable valuation discount to account for the potential losses (i.e., non-accruals).

However, if we look into the details, we will not see as bad picture as it might seem from the surface.

First of all, CION's non-accrual rate remains well below sector average. It is not in the first quartile, but at least it is not above sector average, where, as I said earlier, the average discount is 15% (not 34% as for CION).

Similarly, most of CION's loans are first-lien, which is the safest place to be from the BDC's perspective. More importantly, we have to consider this:

  • 2/3 of PIK loans are structured and not amended. It just confirms that going big on PIK is CION's inherent strategy, and not a byproduct of bad investments. For example, the spread that CION has for its direct strategy investments is 7% over SOFR and for opportunistic cases 14% over SOFR. For a typical core middle market BDC the spread over SOFR is currently around 4.5%. So, through PIK structures CION can both avoid spread compression headwinds and capture outsized yields.
  • About 67% of CION's PIK allocations are risk-rated either 1 or 2 and 98% risk-rated 3 or better. Anything that is below risk-rating 3 (i.e., 4 and 5) is already an indicate of underperforming investments. I think that you will agree with me that the balance here is very favorable.

What was really encouraging to see from Q3 data points was the fact that the NAV per share surged higher. The NAV increased from $14.50 per share in Q2 to $14.86 in Q3. The increase was driven by accretive share repurchases, unrealized appreciation and, importantly, realized (monetized) PIK investments, which drove the NII per share from $0.32 in Q2 to $0.74 in Q3. Had the PIK monetization not happened, the NII per share would have landed at the same level as in the prior quarter, which would support ~13% dividend yield (excluding NAV-funded component).

All of this is a clear proof that the market's fears about CION's NAV sustainability are overblown.

In my humble opinion, it will be a gradual process for CION's share price to converge closer to the NAV. It will take additional quarters like Q3 to convince the market. In the meantime, we can enjoy a compelling yield of almost 15%.
